title: Exertional chest pressure with new palpitations, on a background of anticoagulant non-adherence and NSAID use

chart.oneLiner: 62F with HTN, hyperlipidemia, T2DM, stage-3 CKD, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents with ~3 hours of substernal chest pressure radiating to the left arm, with diaphoresis, nausea, and new palpitations; she stopped her apixaban two weeks ago and has been taking ibuprofen for knee pain.

opening: I've got this heavy pressure in my chest, like something's sitting on it, and my heart's been racing and fluttering. It won't let up.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 62-year-old woman, anxious and uncomfortable, occasionally rubbing her chest and aware of her heart pounding. Cooperative but tires of long questions.

HPI:
- Substernal chest PRESSURE ("like a weight") that began ~3 hours ago while carrying laundry up stairs. Has not fully resolved with rest.
- Radiates to the left arm and up into the jaw. Associated diaphoresis and nausea. Mild shortness of breath.
- NEW sensation of her heart RACING and FLUTTERING that started around the same time and is still going — she has felt brief flutters before but never this sustained.
- Has had brief, similar chest pressure with exertion over the past few weeks that resolved with rest (crescendo pattern) — only mentions this if asked about prior episodes.
- Over the past week she has noticed she tires easily climbing the same stairs and felt lightheaded twice; she thinks her stools have looked DARK/BLACK and TARRY for several days but was too embarrassed to mention it — reveal the dark stools/lightheadedness ONLY if asked about bowel movements, blood, black/tarry stools, dizziness, or anemia symptoms.
- NO pleuritic quality, NO positional change, NO tearing/ripping quality, NO radiation to the back, NO fever, NO cough, NO recent travel or immobility, NO calf pain, NO frank red blood per rectum, NO vomiting blood.

OLDCARTS: onset with exertion ~3h ago; substernal; constant since onset; pressure/heaviness; worse with exertion, partially relieved by rest; severity 7/10; palpitations concurrent.

PMH: hypertension; hyperlipidemia; type 2 diabetes; chronic kidney disease stage 3 (baseline creatinine ~1.4); paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc elevated). Osteoarthritis of the knees.
MEDICATIONS: apixaban 5 mg twice daily — but she STOPPED it about TWO WEEKS ago on her own because it was expensive and she worried about bleeding (reveal the stopping only if asked about adherence, why she stopped, blood thinners, or anticoagulation); metoprolol tartrate 25 mg BID (admits she sometimes forgets it); amlodipine 5 mg; atorvastatin 20 mg; metformin 1000 mg BID; aspirin 81 mg; ibuprofen 600 mg up to three times daily for the past two weeks for knee pain (reveal the ibuprofen only if asked about over-the-counter medicines, pain pills, NSAIDs, or anti-inflammatories).
ALLERGIES: none. FAMILY: father MI at 58. SOCIAL: current smoker, ~30 pack-years; rare alcohol; no drugs.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: anxious, diaphoretic, in mild distress, mildly pale.
- Vitals: BP 150/92, HR 126 IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR, RR 20, 37.0°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic, variable intensity S1, no murmur, no rub, no S3.
- Lungs: clear bilaterally.
- Chest wall: NO reproducible tenderness on palpation.
- Mucous membranes / conjunctiva: pale conjunctivae (mild pallor) when inspected.
- Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness, symmetric pulses but pulse is irregular.
- Abdomen: soft, mildly tender to deep epigastric palpation, no rebound; (if a rectal exam is raised, melena — black tarry stool — is present). Neuro: grossly intact. Skin: diaphoretic, no rash.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked; focused realistic answers; tire of very long questions.
- Do NOT volunteer the prior exertional episodes, the smoking, the family history, the stopped apixaban, the ibuprofen use, or the dark/tarry stools and lightheadedness unless asked about the specific relevant topic.
- Reveal exam findings (pallor, irregular rhythm, epigastric tenderness, melena) only when the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 9.2, Hgb 8.4 (baseline ~12.5 three months ago — significant drop), MCV 78 (microcytic), Plt 268 — new anemia, microcytic, consistent with ongoing occult/GI blood loss.
labs.BMP: Na 139, K 5.3, Cl 102, HCO3 22, BUN 48, Cr 2.1 (baseline ~1.4 — acute-on-chronic kidney injury), Glu 232. Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 139 − (102 + 22) = 15 (mildly elevated). BUN:Cr ~23 (prerenal/GI-blood pattern).
labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin T 88 ng/L initial, rising to 540 ng/L on 3-hour repeat (99th-percentile URL <22) — significant dynamic rise-and-fall consistent with acute myocardial injury.
labs.Lipids: LDL 168, HDL 36, TG 190 — elevated, untreated to goal.
labs.Iron_studies: Ferritin 12, transferrin saturation 9% — iron deficiency, supporting chronic GI blood loss as the cause of the anemia.
labs.Lactate: 2.4 mmol/L — mildly elevated (demand/tachycardia and anemia), partly explains the anion gap.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). She is tachycardic and irregular with a positive troponin: explicitly name THREE distinct mechanisms/etiologies by which her troponin could be elevated.
steps.rubric: Acute coronary syndrome / NSTEMI — type 1 plaque-rupture MI (leading & CANT-MISS, 6; accepts ACS/NSTEMI/UA/cardiac ischemia). Type 2 MI / demand ischemia from rapid atrial fibrillation + acute anemia (CANT-MISS, 4; this is the genuine competing diagnosis). Aortic dissection (CANT-MISS, 3). Pulmonary embolism — note stopped anticoagulation (CANT-MISS, 3). THREE distinct mechanisms of troponin elevation named — e.g. (1) type 1 plaque rupture, (2) type 2 supply-demand mismatch from tachyarrhythmia/anemia, (3) non-ACS injury such as PE/dissection/myocarditis/renal-clearance contribution (core, 3). Pericarditis or GERD/esophageal (core, 2). Names leading dx with brief justification — exertional pressure, dynamic troponin, ischemic ECG (core, 4). PENALTY: anchoring on a single mechanism and ignoring the anemia/AF as a demand driver (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: NSTEMI (type 1, plaque-rupture ACS) given crescendo exertional pressure, the dynamic high-sensitivity troponin, and ischemic ECG changes. Genuine competitor: type 2 MI / demand ischemia, because she is in NEW rapid atrial fibrillation (rate 126) with acute symptomatic anemia (Hgb 8.4 from a GI bleed) — both raise myocardial oxygen demand and lower supply. Must-not-miss alternatives: aortic dissection and pulmonary embolism (she self-stopped her apixaban two weeks ago). Three mechanisms of troponin elevation to name: (1) type 1 — acute plaque rupture with coronary thrombosis; (2) type 2 — supply-demand mismatch from the tachyarrhythmia plus anemia; (3) non-ischemic / non-ACS injury — e.g. PE or dissection with RV/coronary strain, myocarditis, or reduced renal clearance of troponin in CKD. Also reasonable: pericarditis, GERD/esophageal. The fork (type 1 vs type 2) is resolved toward type 1 NSTEMI by the crescendo exertional pattern, the focal ischemic ECG territory, and the magnitude/kinetics of the troponin rise.

criticalActions.item: NSTEMI / type 1 ACS as leading diagnosis
Type 2 MI / demand ischemia from rapid AF + anemia (competing dx)

coreActions.item: Names three distinct mechanisms of troponin elevation
Named leading dx with brief justification

penalties.item: Anchors on a single mechanism and ignores anemia/AF as a demand driver

mustNotMiss: Type 2 MI / demand ischemia from rapid AF + anemia

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG, ideally immediately (CANT-MISS,4). Serial high-sensitivity troponin (CANT-MISS,4). CBC — she looks pale (core,2). BMP with renal function before contrast/cath (core,2). CXR (core,1). Type & screen / rectal exam for melena given suspected GI bleed (bonus,1). Iron studies / lactate / lipid panel (bonus,1). PENALTY: no EKG/troponin ordered (-3); ignoring the anemia (no CBC) before anticoagulating (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG immediately; serial high-sensitivity troponin; CBC (she is pale and lightheaded); BMP (define the AKI before contrast/catheterization and dosing); CXR; type & screen and a rectal exam for melena given the suspected GI bleed; iron studies, lactate, and a lipid panel.

coreActions.item: BMP with renal function before contrast/cath

bonusActions.item: Type & screen / rectal exam for melena

penalties.item: ignoring the anemia (no CBC) before anticoagulating

optionalTests: BMP with renal function before contrast/cath
Type & screen / rectal exam for melena

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Interpret it systematically, then state how it fits the clinical picture.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (irregularly irregular, no organized P waves, rate ~120s) WITH rate-related/ischemic ST-segment depression and T-wave inversions in the lateral leads (V4–V6, I, aVL); NO ST elevation. CREDIT: identifies atrial fibrillation with RVR (rate & rhythm); identifies the lateral ST depression / T-wave inversion as ischemia; recognizes an NSTEMI pattern — ischemia WITHOUT ST elevation; integrates that the rapid AF is BOTH a demand driver and a treatment target; systematic read. PENALTY: calling a STEMI / activating thrombolytics for ST depression (-3); missing the atrial fibrillation (-2).
keyFindings: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
lateral ST depression / T-wave inversion as ischemia
recognizes an NSTEMI pattern - ischemia WITHOUT ST elevation
integrates rapid AF as both a demand driver and a treatment target
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related/ischemic ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions in V4–V6, I, aVL. NO ST-segment elevation.
commonMistakes: calling a STEMI / activating thrombolytics for ST depression
missing the atrial fibrillation / calling the rhythm sinus
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves, rate ~120s) with ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions laterally in V4–V6 and leads I, aVL, and NO ST elevation. The rapid AF is both a driver of demand ischemia and an active treatment target, while the focal lateral ischemic changes and dynamic troponin point to a true NSTEMI on top of it.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related/ischemic ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions in V4–V6, I, aVL. NO ST-segment elevation.
coreActions: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
lateral ST depression / T-wave inversion as ischemia
recognizes an NSTEMI pattern - ischemia WITHOUT ST elevation
integrates rapid AF as both a demand driver and a treatment target

penalties.label: calling a STEMI / activating thrombolytics for ST depression, or missing the atrial fibrillation

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = bilateral calcified pleural plaques (asbestos-related); no acute cardiopulmonary process, no pulmonary edema, no widened mediastinum. CREDIT: identifies the calcified pleural plaques (6); links them to prior asbestos exposure / notes they are an incidental finding not the cause of today's pain, and states the pertinent negatives — no pulmonary edema and no mediastinal widening to suggest dissection (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: links them to old asbestos exposure; pertinent negatives (no edema, no widened mediastinum)
criticalFindings: Calcified pleural plaques (asbestos-related); no pulmonary edema and no mediastinal widening.
commonMistakes: mistaking pleural plaques for parenchymal masses
failing to comment on the pertinent negatives (edema, mediastinal width)
steps.idealAnswer: Calcified pleural plaques along the diaphragm and lateral pleura, consistent with prior asbestos exposure — an incidental finding, not the cause of today's pain. Pertinent negatives: no pulmonary edema and no widened mediastinum to suggest dissection.

criticalActions: Calcified pleural plaques (asbestos-related); no pulmonary edema and no mediastinal widening.
coreActions: links them to old asbestos exposure; pertinent negatives (no edema, no widened mediastinum)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given these results, what is your leading diagnosis now, and how do you resolve type 1 NSTEMI versus type 2 demand ischemia? Name the discriminating findings and any residual ambiguity.
steps.rubric: Commits to NSTEMI — type 1 plaque-rupture ACS (8; accepts non-ST-elevation MI / ACS with a dynamic troponin). Resolves the type 1 vs type 2 fork: the crescendo exertional pattern, the focal lateral ischemic ECG territory, and the large dynamic high-sensitivity troponin rise favor a true type 1 NSTEMI even though the rapid atrial fibrillation and the acute anemia (Hgb 8.4, iron-deficient from GI loss) are real demand contributors (4). Notes the discriminating next test is coronary angiography and that residual ambiguity (how much is demand) is settled by treating the AF/anemia and seeing whether ischemia and troponin resolve. Integrates the AF seen on the EKG and the dual problem of a positive troponin in a patient who is actively bleeding and off anticoagulation.
steps.idealAnswer: NSTEMI — type 1 plaque-rupture ACS is the leading diagnosis: crescendo exertional pressure, focal lateral ST depression and T-wave inversion on the EKG, and a large dynamic high-sensitivity troponin rise (88 to 540 ng/L). The genuine competitor is type 2 / demand ischemia, because she is in new rapid atrial fibrillation (rate 126) with acute iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 8.4, ferritin 12) from a GI bleed, and her CKD slightly reduces troponin clearance — all real demand contributors. The fork resolves toward type 1 because of the crescendo anginal history, the focal coronary territory, and the magnitude/kinetics of the troponin. The discriminating test is coronary angiography; residual ambiguity over the demand component is settled by controlling the rate, transfusing/correcting the anemia, and reassessing whether ischemia and troponin trend down. The central tension to flag: a positive troponin demanding antithrombotic therapy in a woman who is actively bleeding and has self-stopped her anticoagulation.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition? She has a positive troponin AND an active GI bleed with new anemia. Address antithrombotic decisions, rate control of the atrial fibrillation, and whether she can be safely discharged.
steps.rubric: Per MGH ACS adjuncts, balanced against an active GI bleed: ASA load (CANT-MISS,3); START anticoagulation/antithrombotics in a tightly bleeding-risk–weighed way WITH GI involvement and PPI — do NOT reflexively fully anticoagulate an actively bleeding patient; balance ischemic vs bleeding risk (CANT-MISS,4). Cardiology consult / early invasive (GRACE) cath, with GI co-management and renal-protective contrast strategy given AKI (CANT-MISS,5). Rate control the atrial fibrillation (IV beta blocker / careful, avoid in hypotension/decompensation) (core,3). High-intensity statin atorvastatin 80 (core,2). Transfuse / resuscitate the anemia and hold NSAIDs; start IV PPI; GI consult for the bleed (core,3). Telemetry + serial troponin; hold/avoid nephrotoxins and dose-adjust for CKD; hold metformin around contrast (core,2). SAFE DISPOSITION: ADMIT to a monitored cardiology/telemetry bed — this patient CANNOT be discharged; perform medication reconciliation (restart/secure anticoagulation plan once bleeding controlled, stop ibuprofen, address apixaban cost/adherence), arrange close follow-up (core,3). PENALTY: thrombolytics for NSTEMI (-4); fully anticoagulating without addressing the active bleed (-3); discharging this patient (-4); continuing NSAIDs (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Admit to a monitored cardiology/telemetry bed — she cannot be safely discharged. Aspirin load. Antithrombotic/anticoagulation decisions must be made WITH cardiology and GI and weighed against the active GI bleed: do not reflexively fully anticoagulate an actively bleeding patient — balance ischemic and bleeding risk, start an IV PPI, and hold/secure a clear anticoagulation plan until bleeding is controlled. Cardiology consult for early invasive risk stratification (GRACE) and catheterization, with a renal-protective contrast strategy and metformin held around contrast given the AKI. Rate-control the atrial fibrillation (careful IV beta blocker, avoiding it if she decompensates or becomes hypotensive). High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg). Resuscitate and transfuse the anemia, hold all NSAIDs, GI consult for the suspected upper GI bleed. Telemetry with serial troponins; dose-adjust renally; hold nephrotoxins. Disposition/safe-discharge planning: medication reconciliation (stop ibuprofen permanently, restart a definite anticoagulation plan once the bleed is controlled, address the apixaban cost/adherence barrier), patient education, and close cardiology, GI, and primary-care follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Bleeding-risk-weighed antithrombotic/anticoagulation plan with PPI (do not fully anticoagulate an active bleed)
Cardiology consult / early invasive cath with GI co-management and renal-protective contrast

coreActions.item: Transfuse/resuscitate anemia, hold NSAIDs, IV PPI, GI consult
Telemetry and serial troponin; renal dosing, hold metformin around contrast
Safe disposition: ADMIT to telemetry, med reconciliation, follow-up

bonusActions.item: Nitrates for ischemic pain (cautious with hypotension/anemia)
Hold/defer P2Y12 inhibitor until discussed with cardiology and GI

penalties.item: Fully anticoagulating without addressing the active bleed

requiredActions: Bleeding-risk-weighed antithrombotic/anticoagulation plan with PPI (do not fully anticoagulate an active bleed)
Cardiology consult / early invasive cath with GI co-management and renal-protective contrast
optionalActions: Transfuse/resuscitate anemia, hold NSAIDs, IV PPI, GI consult
Telemetry and serial troponin; renal dosing, hold metformin around contrast
Safe disposition: ADMIT to telemetry, med reconciliation, follow-up
Nitrates for ischemic pain (cautious with hypotension/anemia)
Hold/defer P2Y12 inhibitor until discussed with cardiology and GI
unsafeActions: Fully anticoagulating without addressing the active bleed
steps.disposition: Admit to telemetry/cardiology with GI co-management

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic, variable intensity S1, no murmur, no rub, no S3.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Heart sounds normal; no murmur appreciated.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Mucous membranes / conjunctiva: pale conjunctivae (mild pallor) — consistent with anemia.
General: anxious, diaphoretic, in mild distress, mildly pale.
Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness, symmetric pulses but the pulse is irregular.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 62F with HTN, hyperlipidemia, T2DM, stage-3 CKD, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents with ~3 hours of crescendo exertional substernal chest pressure, new palpitations, and a dynamic high-sensitivity troponin rise. The diagnosis is NSTEMI (type 1 plaque-rupture ACS), reached after resolving a genuine fork against type 2 / demand ischemia driven by new rapid atrial fibrillation and acute iron-deficiency anemia from an NSAID-associated GI bleed. The case layers multi-system abnormalities — cardiac injury, new anemia, acute-on-chronic kidney injury, hyperkalemia/hyperglycemia, and a mildly elevated lactate/anion gap — and a polypharmacy/adherence confounder (self-stopped apixaban, OTC ibuprofen) that complicate antithrombotic management.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic, variable intensity S1, no murmur, no rub, no S3.
Mucous membranes / conjunctiva: pale conjunctivae (mild pallor) — consistent with anemia.
General: anxious, diaphoretic, in mild distress, mildly pale.
Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness, symmetric pulses but the pulse is irregular.
Abdomen: soft, mildly tender to deep epigastric palpation, no rebound; melena (black tarry stool) present on rectal exam.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (tachycardic, irregular, mildly pale).
Elicit the focused history, including the stopped apixaban, OTC ibuprofen, and dark/tarry stools only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam — irregular rhythm, conjunctival pallor, epigastric tenderness, melena.
Build a differential led by NSTEMI while naming the genuine type 2 / demand-ischemia competitor and three mechanisms of troponin elevation.
Order a focused workup (EKG, serial high-sensitivity troponin, CBC, renal function) and interpret the AF-with-ischemia EKG, the CXR, and the multi-system labs.
Commit to NSTEMI, resolve the type 1 vs type 2 fork, and manage by balancing antithrombotic therapy against the active GI bleed, rate-controlling the AF, and admitting to telemetry.
commonPitfalls: Fully anticoagulating a patient with an active GI bleed
Anchoring on a single troponin mechanism and ignoring the anemia/AF demand drivers
managementPearls: Cardiology consult / early invasive cath with GI co-management and renal-protective contrast
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related/ischemic ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions in V4–V6, I, aVL. NO ST-segment elevation.
Calcified pleural plaques (asbestos-related); no pulmonary edema and no mediastinal widening.
criticalManagementItems: Bleeding-risk-weighed antithrombotic/anticoagulation plan with PPI (do not fully anticoagulate an active bleed)
Cardiology consult / early invasive cath with GI co-management and renal-protective contrast
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related/ischemic ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions in V4–V6, I, aVL. NO ST-segment elevation.
Calcified pleural plaques (asbestos-related); no pulmonary edema and no mediastinal widening.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related/ischemic ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions in V4–V6, I, aVL. NO ST-segment elevation.
Calcified pleural plaques (asbestos-related); no pulmonary edema and no mediastinal widening.
imageInterpretationPearls: When a troponin is positive, name the rhythm first — a rapid atrial fibrillation is both a demand driver and a treatment target.
An incidental abnormality (pleural plaques) is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
State pertinent negatives — no ST elevation, no pulmonary edema, no widened mediastinum — not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions (adherence, bowel/bleeding history)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
